Okay, so when we put together the hard building day, you know, and when we think about a guy who has worked really hard to build a list that is a ultra-responsive list, there's a few people's names that are on that list. It's a short list. Two next people that you have certainly epitomize that, two next people that will be up here speaking. The first one is Frank Kern, the second one is Ryan Dice. Both of them really get this, so we tapped into our friends and members and we figured out let's embarrass them and make them feel guilty in coming here. Although I don't think it's possible to embarrass Frank, but hey, listen, maybe you guys can do that. So with no further ado, you had a lot of time with him yesterday. He doesn't really require any kind of introduction at all. The guy who is known as Not Tony, Not Tony Robbins, so I just call him Not Tony, okay? Mr. Not Tony, Frank Kern, here we go. I just don't walk fast, sorry. I move slow. Last time I'd be walking slower than that even. Don't rush me, man. Okay, take your time. It's 7.30 in the morning. Hey everybody, how's it going? This is when I could use the whiteboard for legitimate shit. Does this thing work? Do, ro, that thing on? I don't need it right now, but I will, maybe. What am I supposed to talk about, Bill? List getting? Stuff? You're going to talk about whatever you want to talk about anyway, so knock yourself out. Okay. Whatever could we talk about? All right, so I got some new list building stuff that's working pretty good for me. The thing to thank you very much, I can get away with anything in this Ryan Dice name bench here. How about this? Fuck all y'all. I'm Ryan Dice, bitches. I'm going to say, wait a minute. I'm Ryan Dice, bitch. Okay. What are we talking about? Oh yeah, list. So I've discovered some stuff that's working pretty good for me. The things that you have learned through the GKIC world have worked for everybody forever. This stuff is working really well for me, and I'm just now testing it. So it's very new. I don't know if it universally works, but my suspicion is that it will, so I'd like to share it with you just to give you new stuff for me, because I like to hear new stuff myself. So I have determined that not all lists are created equal. I determine that by fucking stuff up really bad and not making money on certain promotions because I've used the wrong list. So you can buy lists or you can attract lists, right? And as we have learned a gazillion times, it's like never really the thing or the creative or the offer as much as it is the list or the market. So what I'd like to talk to you about today is not really how to build a huge list, but how to build a list that buys stuff rapidly, and then how to attract that list in a way that psychologically sets them up to already know and trust you before you have any interaction with them. Cool? All right. Super. So here's the deal. The way that I used to go about market research or market getting or hunting or whatever was the classic way that's worked forever, which is find out what people want and then give it to them. All right? So the criteria that I learned from market selection is, I think I learned it from Jeff Paul and then later ripped it off and said fuck a lot and claimed it as my own, but it was basically, what was it? You have to be irrationally passionate, great in number and easily reached. That was like, excuse me, that was like the market criteria for what you should be after. Yeah? And it worked great. Made me a lot of money. I taught it. Made other people a lot of money. It was fantastic. The problem with it is it kind of can be a little bit too broad and you can end up having things be a pain in the ass. I'll give you some statistics here later. So I have created a new formula in my business that's working exceptionally well and it begins with the selection of the market and a good market with what I've been doing has to have four criteria. It has to meet four very, very critical, non-replaceable, non-negotiable criteria. The first thing, and this isn't step number one. This is a given that whatever it is you're selling has to be of massive transformational value. And that really is the result that your stuff delivers has to be of massive transformational value. I like to sell expensive stuff because when you sell expensive stuff you can spend a lot of money to acquire the sale and when you can spend a lot of money to acquire the sale you can market your ass off where other people cannot. You can market more aggressively. So I like to sell things now in the eight to twelve thousand dollar range. So when you're doing that, the result of your information has to be of massive transformational value. So when we sell information we're almost always selling change or improvement in our little world. Like I said yesterday, no one's going to buy a course on how to make your life worse or how to lose money or how to get fatter. Actually, Sumo wrestlers might, you know, or how to screw up your children somehow or whatever, how to get even worse on drugs. So we're always trying to have improvement. So when you're examining what it is you're selling you want the outcome of the information and if your customers applied it properly to be something that would massively transform their lives for the better, this justifies your higher price. So an example of that would be I worked with someone who sold addiction recovery stuff and this guy had a way to get people to stop smoking cracker or whatever quickly instead of them going to rehab 20 times and all of that cracker. So that's massive transformational value. I mean the guy takes his stuff, he quit smoking cracker, his life has immediately changed all as well. How to make more money, how to get more patients, whatever massive transformational value, right? You save the business from going under, guy feeds his family, big deal. How to change a light bulb, not so transformation. Yeah, you get it? Okay. So we are taking that as a given with these criteria. So with that said, our first thing that the market has to meet, the first requirement that the market has to meet is that they must immediately and easily recognize the value of what it is you're selling. They have to immediately and easily recognize the value of what you are selling and I'll give you the example from which I learned this, aka School of Hard Knocks. So I started a company over the summer and we were selling done for you internet marketing. So we would go to small businesses and we would build them a direct response website with all their information on there. Then we would get them, we'd write their Google ads and we would manage their Google campaigns and we would pay for their traffic and we would just handle it for them because they didn't want to fuck with it. So we started out, oh here it is, a little water, we started out and being the genius that I am with a capital J, we decided to go after plumbers because I just literally pulled plumbers out of my ass. I was like, hey fuck it, let's try plumbers. So I bought a list of 30 something thousand plumbers and got myself a partner and built a phone room and we started cold calling these plumbers. Basically saying, do you want to buy or shit or whatever. And we did okay but not nearly as well as I thought we would do and I was like, what in the hell is wrong? These guys want this stuff. I would write ads and they'd respond, yes I want to get more plumbing customers and all this kind of stuff and I was just like, man this is some weird shit. So finally after talking to like plumber number 35,000 or something, I had one of the guys in the phone room and I was like, hey man, ask them how much each new customer is worth to them. So they did and we started asking that and the numbers that they were feeding back to us were each new customer was worth on average about 500 bucks. And now our thing of massive transformational value that we were selling was we could deliver on average without like puffery about 10 new customers a month just doing some ad words for them and you know, just basic internet marketing stuff, nothing fancy, no launches, just here's your shit, you know, here's a good website instead of a stupid one. So we would tell them that and we say, yeah, you know, we could probably get you about 10 customers a month, it's not going to, you know, it's not going to promise you a million of them but it turns better than none, you know, recession, blah, blah, blah. So I started doing the math and what I learned was so 10 new customers a month at $500 a customer is $5,000 increase in revenue per month for that plumber which on the surface looks great. All right, so then he's always like, well, what the hell, how does that all really shake out? So the plumber almost literally has to wade through shit to make the $5,000, right? And then he's got to pay for the trucks and pay for the meth head assistant he's got and pay the workman's comp and all this kind of shit. At the end of the day that $5,000 probably turns into, you know, $1,500. So we're asking him to take a chance. That's criteria number one for those of you at home who are following along. We're asking him to take a chance on us strangers over the telephone and give us around the $500 a month was our service, what we cost, around $500 a month to ultimately make another $1,500 if we were not lying, right? So while he was like, I mean, it's a little bit more money, he didn't immediately recognize it. You follow me? So then I was like, well, shit, no wonder we're not having a good run with this. So then we decided to switch to chiropractors. So we take the call on the chiropractors and we start off asking them what their average patient was worth. And they told us that they calculated this by the year, interestingly enough, where the plumber did not. I guess the chiropractors are more sophisticated. They said the average patient was worth between $6,000 and $30,000 a year. This is what they told us. I don't know if that's statistically accurate for the chiropractors here, but that's what our guys on the phone told us. So go back to what we're offering. Our magic did not change. We could get them about 10 new patients, just like we could get the plumber about 10 new dudes with broken toilets. You're not doing anything different. But now, when we look at this, we are able to provide times 10 at the low side every month that's an additional $60,000 worth of revenue for the year that we added to this guy's bottom line. You follow me so far? The guy's making $6,000 per patient as opposed to $500 per customer. So he was immediately more receptive than the chiropractors were. Why? Because we immediately recognized the transformational value of what we had to offer. We didn't change. Our stuff did not change. Our prices went up. We tripled them, actually. Charged a lot more money and our conversion rates went up. So that's thing number one when you're selecting the herd that you want to build. You start targeting the right people and you have to really, really narrow it down. Thing number two is, and everybody knows this, but I'm going to say it anyway, because I, too, have forgotten it on many, many occasions. They have to be able to afford what it is that you have to offer. So that's obvious, right? Like don't sell to broke people. But there's a word I'd like you to write down in front of the word afford, and that is easily. You want to target people in your herd who can easily afford what it is that you have to offer back to our tale of woe and tragedy narrowly averted with the company over the summer. A little horse this morning. I think it's the cold air. Thanks for bearing with me. I didn't party last night, by the way. It was good. I tried to watch that movie with Cameron Diaz and Tom Cruise. It's horrible. You guys see that? Were Tom Cruise like this rogue secret agent or something? I turned it off, even though I had Cameron Diaz in it. So anyway, easily afford. So let's go back to plumbers, right? So they're like, yeah, we make 500 bucks of customers. Okay, some of them bought, whatever. Turns out from our research, I don't know if it's correct because it's us, but our research was saying that the plumbers average guys make about 60 to 80 grand a year. Now our stuff is about 500 bucks a month, $6,000 a year for the plumber, right? That's 10% of his income. So can he afford it? Yes. Can he easily afford it? No. So we switch over to the chiropractors. They make a lot more money easily afford, right? If we were really smart, we would have targeted some sort of a cosmetic surgeon or something whose customer or patient value was a lot higher who made shitloads of money. So easily afford. You want to filter all this stuff out before you even start because what good is a shitload of leads if you can't convert them? If they're the wrong leads, you're wasting your money or lists or herd or whatever you want to refer to the mask. So piece of criteria number three because criteria now come in pieces is I totally forgot. Oh yes, apply. And this is something I bet you ain't never heard before unless you've taken a class from me and my man Kevin. So this is directly attributable to us specifically as information marketers and probably nobody ever discusses this because they're not weirdos like me who stay up all night thinking about this kind of shit. But here's the thing. Whenever we make a major decision, whether it's a buying decision or a relationship decision or any like big deal decision, we play a movie in our heads of what the outcome of that decision is likely to produce. We don't consciously do this. It's just a reaction. So you've probably done it. You've contemplated getting a new car and you like envision yourself in the car. You imagine your friend's reaction to your decision to purchase the car. You do it when we all do it. We buy a house. You envision you're sitting around the little breakfast nook with the kids or whatever, building your bondage and domination room in the basement, Dan or whatever it is that you want to do. And Bill, okay, let's keep everyone included. Mainly Bill. Not there's anything wrong with that. Not if that's who you are. So we all play those movies. The same thing happens with information product purchases, especially when they're expensive. So you have two types of people who are going to buy your stuff. People who are likely to apply your information and people who are not. Let's start with those who are not likely to apply and why they are bad. First of all, they're bad for business because if they don't apply your information, what are they not going to get ever? Exactly. So if they don't get any results, what are they unlikely to ever do? Buy a yo shit. Right. So it doesn't matter if you have the best information ever and it is presented in a solid gold briefcase that like when you open it, the light comes out like in Pulp Fiction. You know, it has no bearing on you. If you're selling to people who do not apply your shit, they won't get any results. That's bad thing number one about them. Bad thing number two about them is that you will ultimately get weirded out because if you attract people who aren't likely to apply and they don't get results, at least this happened to me, you're like, should have said me? Is it my stuff? Am I not doing it right? No one's doing anything. You know, and it'll just fuck you up psychologically and you'll feel weirded out and I'll make you not want to sell to them anymore because it's just no fun. That's kind of why I work with small business now because they do shit. You know what I mean? So thing number three and most significant about those who are unlikely to apply versus those who are likely to apply is the movie that is played in their head. So someone who is unlikely to apply plays a movie where the starring role of that movie is their own fantasy, their own sense of fiction. And we've all probably had a similar experience whether it's with an information product or exercise device or, you know, diet book or whatever it may be, right? So you're contemplating the decision and you play the movie in your head and we'll do it with a make money course. You play the movie in your head, the course comes, you open it, the golden light shines out like in pulp fiction. No work ever occurs. The middle of the movie is like kind of cloudy and at the end of the day, based on pure hope, somehow it works miraculously. Money falls from the sky. You drive off in the convertible with the cheerleader from high school, your high school, not an actual high school cheerleader, but the one that you liked in high school, giving the finger to all those who mocked you in the past. You know, and that's like, that's the movie that is played by those who are unlikely to apply and it's all fiction and they know that it's fiction. They know it's fiction. They know it's based on hope, but occasionally they will buy anyway because it feels good in the moment to satiate that desire and the act of purchasing makes them feel like they're doing something productive, right? So then they buy and then they don't do anything and then they either say, you're a horrible person because they didn't open your course or do anything and somehow you prevented them from doing it like you put a Jedi lock on it, you know, or whatever. And then they don't buy any more of your shit. And now let's take the flip side to those who are likely to apply, right? Those who are likely to apply aside from all the other stuff, which is they get results and they buy more of your shit and they're just awesome, they play an entirely different movie. They play a movie that involves them utilizing your stuff. So a lot of you are here because I know a lot of you personally, when you get a sales letter from Dan and Bill about a new thing and it talks about like a new campaign, like the S&D campaigns or whatever, you envision yourself writing that shit out and you say, I'm going to run this Thanksgiving campaign to this group of buyers and it's probably going to produce this result and you're already thinking about the outcome, thinking about how you're going to apply it, right? You ever had that experience? So what happens when you're having that experience is you're pre-selling yourself before any real salesmanship on the part of the vendor occurs. So those who are likely to apply are almost pre-sold before you have to deal with them at all, which is great because there's no reason in the world to build a herd of people who aren't going to buy your shit, right? So we want to attract those who are pre-sold. So now we have, we're attracting people who immediately recognize, no brainer recognize the transformational value of what you have to offer, right? Who can easily afford it and who are likely to apply it. Do you think that they're likely to buy it at this stage without any miraculous salesman ability on your part? Of course they are. Ah, but we are. That's not done. There is one final piece of criteria-lism. There's a new one. Invented that word, thank you. Criterion-ness and that is benefit. And benefit is married to happily but somewhat dysfunctionally to recognize. And here's the deal. Want to attract and target those who are most likely to benefit the most right now from your stuff. We'll go back to the summer debacle, okay? Started out with plumbers, not so great, right? They didn't recognize how cool we were because at the end of the day we're going to maybe, we're not lying, make them $1,500 net profit after they deal with a bunch of grumpy house wives, crawl through human feces and pay their meth addict assistant. So they're like, ah, this is kind of stupid. So then we target chiropractors and they're like, yeah, this is pretty good. This will probably work even if these guys get me two patients a month. It's still worth it. Fuck it, I'll give it a shot. That works okay. Now, who would benefit the most in the small business marketplace? Probably someone whose average customer or patient value was huge, right? Like a cosmetic surgeon or something. And this is by no means me saying go after cosmetic surgeons. I haven't tested that market, so I don't know. But someone like them whose average patient or customer value over the course of a year could be $25,000 to $100,000. They're most likely to benefit the most from what we have to offer right now. I haven't changed the offer, have I? At all. I've just changed the sites that I'm pointing the gun at. You understand? So those are the four criteria of a good market that you want to go after. It works to find someone who's a rationally passionate, great number and easily reached, but it works a lot better to find people who meet these criteria. Your target may be smaller, but your ROI per lead generated is much greater. So I'm kind of burning through this. This is great. I'm going to have an early lunch. All right. You all with me so far? I know it's Sunday and we're like doing work and stuff. I'm not telling as many jokes, but I really want you to get results and make money as a result of our experience together. So thus the serious tone of the teaching. So now that we have our target, we got to attract them right. So the model of lead attraction that I learned that worked and served me well, I have several million reasons on my bank statement to be grateful to this lead attraction method. It is the one that's been around forever. And that is bribe them into coming into your funnel by offering them cool, free shit. All right. This worked great for me. It's probably great for a lot of you guys. The old free report reveals something cool. Yeah. Enter your email address here. Free report reveals awesome shit. Call this number for a 24 hour free recorded greeting. I actually ran one of those old school JP DK ads and it still works to this very day. They still call and leave their number for the 24 hour free recorded greeting, which is great. The problem, if we have to find a problem with that methodology, and that's a big if, if we have to fuck with it and find something wrong with it, is that the people coming into the funnel are coming into your funnel when they respond to that. They're coming in like this because they're like, I'm pretty sure this is a bait and switch. I'm pretty sure this clock is going to try to sell me something. And they're right. We are going to try to sell them. I mean, of course we're going to try to sell them something. And it's not like just us, that model of attracting leads has been around forever. Back in 1904 when Dan was selling life insurance or whatever. Not that any of this is remotely true, by the way. This is the high percentage they say. When you offer the free consultation, I'd like you to come in for a free consultation about your insurance needs. No one's going, okay, how nice of them. I mean, they'll come in, it'll work, but they know there's going to be a pitch and that's okay, it's fine. The model works, it's fucking awesome. But it leads to a lot of effort having to be put into the process of selling. So I'll give you an example from my own launch. The last product that I launched was a product called List Control. And that was launched in the beginning of the year. I really can't remember, that's embarrassing. It was launched earlier in the year. In one of the months that had a vowel in the name, which followed January. In the launch of that product, I generated 80,000, 80 something thousand. It was like 70 something or 80, it was a fucking lot. Between 75 and 85,000 people, I can't remember, on my list, it came into my funnel during that launch. I had the endorsement of every single major player in my marketplace, every single one of them sending out my shit saying, Kern is fucking awesome. I had a video of a dude who was 12 or 13 talking about how much money he had made with my shit. So I was able to be like, look man, I can't guarantee you're going to make money, but Jesus, come on, 12 or 13? Surely you can do better than this guy. I had Tony Robbins saying that I was great. I had affiliates fighting each other to try to sell the most of my stuff by offering huge, great generous bonuses to all those who bought through their links. Despite all of those advantages, I sold 2,000 copies, which was my goal, 2,000 copies. So if you just divide the list, let's say 75,000, that's what, like 3% of the list? I converted it 3% with all of that work, with all of that shit. Best guys, oh this guy's amazing, every single person, I was everywhere. You couldn't turn on the computer in our space without seeing my fucking videos. I'm surprised no one came and shot me. Like look, I like you, I'm just tired of fucking seeing you have to go. So the reason why is because of the way people were brought in, that old school way. That's why when you see launches, which are awesome and they work great, duh, but when you see launches, when you watch the internet marketing space for launches, specifically, what happens, what's the process? You opt in and then you opt in like this going, this is probably going to be a bunch of bullshit. You know, this cocksucker's going to try to sell me something, fuck I don't know. You know, because I'll opt in anyway, because I'm a marketer, but I hate being marketed to, that's just one of the mysteries of the world. You know, isn't that the weirdest thing? Like the people who complain about marketing are marketers, it's like, dude, what are you doing? It's like a cook complaining about food. What's wrong with you? But I digress. All right, so the guy comes in, arms crossed, and then what do we, what do we as marketers in a launch have to do? We have to overcome that by giving tons of free shit away. Like, you know, every launch has like four or five things of cool shit to reverse that opinion. Right, it's all designed to uncross those arms, because when they come in like this, you have to bribe and bribe and cajole and prove that you're not an asshole. Even if they've known you forever, you have to re-prove that you're not an asshole over and over and over again by giving away tons of free shit in this process. So the question becomes, well, what if we just attracted them differently? All right, what if we just had them come into the funnel in a totally different manner with a different mindset, but still using the same methodology? Don't you think that would be more effective? I did, and I tried it, and it worked. So I personally build my lead-generating campaigns now to elicit the three magic words. And you know their magic because they have little lines coming off the circle. That's how you know it's magic, or it's an amoeba. It's one of the two. All right? Doesn't that what an amoeba looks like? I can't remember. I can't believe I remember that. I'm pretty sure that's what an amoeba looks like. So it is not an amoeba? Oh, I was going to say, paramecium. Duh. Thank you. Like, I didn't know that. Paramecium, tattoo, never mind. All right, so here's the deal. Here's why you want to elicit that response instead of, okay, let me see what your free shit is. Would you ever ask someone for help if you did not trust them? No. We're on the side of the road, the cars broke down, you know, there's like an engine smoking your all alone and then like the serial killer van pulls up, you know. And the guy's like, you want a ride? Like, I'm cool. I don't need any help, right? You just can't, like as our internal rules won't let us do it. It's not even a conscious decision. You just won't do it, right? Would you ever ask someone for help if you did not believe they could help you? No. It just doesn't. You can't, it goes against our psychological rules. There's nothing you sit around and think about. Like when you're responding to an ad, you're not like, do I trust this person? Do I believe they can help me? No, you don't like psychologically consciously think about it, but these are the rules that govern our behavior. So why not go with these rules? See, so therefore, if that is true, wouldn't it also be true and isn't it also true that when someone asks us for help, at least on some level, on some level subconsciously or at the little, you know, pinnacle, fine line between consciousness and subconsciousness, don't they trust us a little bit if they ask us for help? Yes or no? Don't they on some level believe that we can help them? All right. So consider this. What are the two biggest reasons people don't buy our shit? I'll tell you. They either don't want it, a duh, or they don't believe us. All right. So assuming we're targeting people who want it, which I know that sounds elementary, but I make the error roughly every 90 days still, because I've only been doing this 11 years. I think 15 years, I'll get over that one. All right. So assuming we're targeting people who want it, if we can eliminate the trust issue right at the point of entry, don't you think we'll sell more shit? This process is leading in my business right now to a 30 to a 20 to 33 percent conversion rate on an $8,000, an $8 to $12,000 product. So it's working very, very good for me. There's a formula. This is going great. I got 21 minutes. I think this is a perfect time. There's a formula that I use to elicit this response in my lead gen. Would you like it? I'll sell it to you. Here's the formula. There's four steps to it. Here's four steps to it. Step number one, you shouldn't do drugs because they're bad. I hadn't seen that show in forever, man. I should have watched it sometime. All right. Let's see here. Actually, there's, I have forgotten how many steps are all we're going to discover them together. There may not be four steps. These results are not typical. Your results may vary. None of the things I'm saying are true at all. At any second, you could explode. Do not do anything. Go home. Eat microwave pizza. All right. And sugar. So step number one is you have to identify the BAP. So BAP is technical jargon. It stands for big ass problem. Every market has a big ass problem. So when we were working with them chiropractors, I took to doing me some of that market research by calling a chiropractor and saying, what's your biggest problem? They said the thing that kept them up at night was acquiring new patients, which was lucky because that's what we were in the business of solving. But sometimes you'll be wrong. Sometimes the big ass problem could have very well been getting the patients to accept the case or whatever. But in that one, it was, in fact, acquiring new patients. So every market is going to have a big ass problem. It's not like you can't force this. You don't want to trick yourself into believing that their big ass problem happens to match whatever you got. Make sure you're accurate in your thinking and your identification with their big ass problem. So first, identify the big ass problem. Step two is to shine a spotlight on the big ass problem. So when I cracked the code on this thing, I wrote a tear sheet ad, which I didn't bring, and I wrote it for chiropractors. The headline said something like 7,243 chiropractic clinics slated to shut their doors. No, slated to be killed in 2010 by bad economy. So I'm shining a huge spotlight on that big ass problem. Bring that right to the front of their mind. Now, already we're different because most of everyone else is selling into our crowd as free report reveals how to get new customers. I haven't talked about new customers yet. I'm entering the conversation in their mind, which is I'm scared I'm going to lose my practice because I ain't getting enough patience. Okay? Now, you probably know these first two steps already from basic copywriting, but what you might not have done, been explained to is you have got to take to proven the existence of the big ass problem by featuring third party data because nobody believes us anymore. If they ever did, I don't believe me half the time. It's me. All right? Probably why I don't believe me. You got to prove it. So in that ad, the first thing I did chiropractors at the dinner table, he gets my tear sheet sent lovingly from Jay. You guys mail tear sheets. It's the greatest thing. I love it. Fake newspaper articles. Anyway, he's at the dinner table. You're 7,000 something chiropractic clinics slated to be killed in 2010 by bad economy. The first paragraph says that according to research done by independent firm BizMiner, 40.02% of new chiropractic clinics fail within their first year. Backed it up, didn't I? The old method or the less sophisticated method, which still works okay, would be to go, you got a real big problem. Here's how bad your problem is without proving it. You've got to prove it and it can never, ever be because you said so. It has to be proven by third party data. It's easy to get the third party data. You just type in like insert whatever here and then statistics and you can find the fucking data or you can go to yahooanswers.com and ask. And people will tell you, I don't know why they do this. They just do. They like to answer weird shit. They go there and say, give me scary statistics about XYZ and they'll give them to you. But you got to prove it. You have to prove it. So the fourth step is to twist the knife. What was that Dennis? That Dennis Leary had that comedy sketch. I'm an asshole. I'm a silly odio. Well, you kind of have to be an asshole in this one. But you're helping them at the end of the day. So they need to wake the fuck up. They have a big ass problem in a serious and you're doing them a favor by smacking them around and saying, hey, come on. So you twist the knife. So in the formula to give you an example of how I did it in the ad, I started out, oh, let me tell you where I got the number. Does anyone know where I got the number and the headline from? Exactly. So when you use data, like make it real, never make this shit up. So my headline said 7,242 chiropractors slated to be murdered brutally by the economy in 2010. I got that number by calling my list broker and going, how many chiropractors you got? And he said this many. I said, oh, OK. And I just multiply that by the statistic given to get the number. Make sense? So whenever you can be accurate, accurate, accurate, you got to be pretending you're like a lawyer making a case, right? But actually be accurate. So I go, I start out, shine the spotlight on the big ass problem, prove it immediately. According to the research company, bizminer.com, 40.02% of new chiropractic clinics close or die or whatever within their first year. And I don't remember the number specifically. I'm doing this by memory. The next phase, I've got their attention, don't I? Like that. Not hypey attention, but Jesus Christ, which is what everyone's programmed to respond to anyway because normal people read the news and respond to that kind of scary shit. So you might as well make it work in your favor. So the next step is they're on the edge from complacency to action. I've got to push them over the edge by freaking them out. So what I said in my ad was, it's likely to get worse. Why would I say such a thing? Because it is likely to get worse. So then in the ad, I cited how bad the economy was and how bad it's projected to get. According to, you know, economic forecaster, Harry S Dent, the American economic landscape is plummeting towards an all new low. This could sound the death knell for unprepared chiropractic clinics. You are fucked, dear sir. Wouldn't it be great, you could actually probably do this. I know a guy I've worked with who writes for legitimate magazines and they play a little game where they'll make the first word of every sentence to spell dirty shit and see if they can get away with it. So you could do that in your ad. You could make it say you sir are fucked. Possibly. That would be hilarious. Someone please do that and mail it and just perform. So the fifth step is to show the light. Actually before I explain that, I did more knife twisting in my ad. So I cited how bad the economy was and then I cited that traditional advertising methods were no longer working. I did that because I was selling a new advertising method. This confangled internet fad everyone's talking about. And I knew that when I spoke to them I was going to have to compete against the old stuff they were doing. So now finally, I've spotlighted the big ass problem. I've illustrated to them the severity of the big ass problem as a result of this independent third party data, not just some asshole writing an ad. Then I've scared the shit out of them by telling them the truth, which is it is likely to get significantly worse if they're unprepared. Now that they're all freaked out, now I offer the free report offering the cool shit. So I've just taken the old method and reframed it with some new handy psychology. And the way I did that in this ad was to say, but some chiropractors is having the best year ever. Free report reveals exciting new ways some chiropractors are attracting more customers or more patients in this case than they've ever had if you would like one call XYZ for a free 24 hour pre-recorded message, which of course we are so nice we actually answered the phone in person. We didn't make them leave their message, we took the call and sold them some shit. The end. So that is the formula for generating the proper lead, the person who says I need help. They are no longer as skeptical as the old way where they're like, I know you're going to try to fuck me somehow, you asshole. They're like, please help me, you might still try to fuck me, but I'm really posed, so please help me and then if you help me enough, maybe I'll buy some shit. Make sense? All right, super. So when you take the right market that meets the four criteria that I gave you and then you attract them in the manner which I just explained to you and you are selling something of massive transformational value for a lot of money, you can outmarket what is commonly known as the ever living dog shit out of your competition because you're going to have, at least I've been having and some of our students have been having very high conversion rates. Right? Makes sense. So that is the new formula by which I've been running my business, it's working fabulously. Bill asked me to shave some time off. I have successfully shaved off a lot of time. I have 11 and now 10 minutes and 59 seconds to stand here and ramble at y'all. It's kind of my new list getting stuff which works great. If you have any questions, I'll take them for the next 10 minutes and 50 seconds. Hello. I have a very basic question because I'm very new in information marketing. You're lucky. So, getting into the mail is not something to do half heartedly. So there's an entire universe. These contact lenses are borrowed from my wife or defective. The ability or likelihood to apply, for example, can be determined or better improved by list select. For example, investment and time spent in business and investment made to get in business when you're selling B2B increases the likelihood to apply. So a chiropractor who's been in business for a long time has a lot more vested in his business more likely to apply. A chiropractor who has just bought a new office space more likely to apply because he wants to pay for that office space. A chiropractor who is, I don't know why I'm harping on chiropractic. It's not like it's saturated or anything. But the chiropractor who is actively marketing via display ads in the L pages, more likely to apply, more likely to buy based on those behavioral selects. Now the problem is, and you know that better than I, of course, that in marketing you cannot make money on selling people to be healthier. They only want to spend money once they are ill and they want to get cured. So what I don't think that the big problem for a smoker is, is that all the methods that they use that they fail, and they know that because they tried already several things, and that's why they are so skeptical. So how would you overcome that skepticism, and how would you then build up the value to the smoker to stop smoking? Well, you don't really overcome the skepticism through the ad. You scare the shit out of them and make them ask for help through the ad. So if everyone is saying you're going to die or whatever and it's not working, there's probably and likely another hot button. So I smoked for shit, man, forever. Cigarettes. Forever. I mean, I chewed so much gum that I literally got scar tissue on my tongue and cheeks that I was constantly choking.
